column5: Dublin detective Rob Ryan investigates a child's murder near the woods where he survived a mysterious childhood tragedy.
An agoraphobic woman witnesses something she shouldn't from her New York City apartment. A twisty homage to Hitchcock.
Three women's lives unravel in the lead-up to a school trivia night that ends in murder. Sharp, funny, and devastating.
A famous painter shoots her husband and never speaks again. A criminal psychotherapist is obsessed with uncovering why.
The perfect marriage hides a perfect nightmare. A debut thriller that will make you deeply uncomfortable in the best way.
A daily commuter becomes entangled in the disappearance of a woman she watched from the train. Unreliable narrators at their best.

column1: The Long Way to a Small Angry Planet
column5: A found family crew tunnels wormholes through space. Cozy, humane, and deeply optimistic science fiction.
A man lives in a house of infinite halls and tidal statues, keeping meticulous journals ‚Äî until the mystery of his world begins to crack.

column1: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
column5: The Earth is demolished to make way for a hyperspace bypass. Arthur Dent barely escapes. The funniest sci-fi ever written.
Four people investigate a notoriously haunted mansion. The definitive American haunted house novel ‚Äî quietly terrifying.
A cursed New England girls school in 1900 and a Hollywood film crew in the present day collide in this lush, queer horror novel.
In a world where animals carry a deadly virus, humans have become the new meat. Brutal, brilliant, and unforgettable.
A romance writer and a literary fiction author swap genres for the summer ‚Äî and fall for each other in the process.
Two best friends take a trip every summer. One summer ruined everything. This is the story of trying to get it back.
An econometrician with autism hires an escort to help her practice dating. Warm, sexy, and genuinely moving.
A Harvard sociologist embeds with Milwaukee landlords and tenants to reveal how eviction drives poverty. Pulitzer Prize winner.
What happens when a journalist tries to survive on minimum wage jobs in America? Still essential reading decades later.
A searing argument that mass incarceration is the new racial caste system in America. Changed how millions think about justice.
A woman raised by survivalists in the Idaho mountains educates herself into Cambridge and Harvard. Stunning and harrowing.
Patti Smith's memoir of her formative years with Robert Mapplethorpe in New York City. A love letter to art and friendship.
A neurosurgeon diagnosed with terminal cancer confronts the meaning of life and death. Devastating and beautiful.
The epic story of the Great Migration ‚Äî Black Americans who fled the South between 1915 and 1970. Masterful narrative history.
The last voyage of the Lusitania in 1915, told in real time. Larson at his most gripping.
The 1893 Chicago World's Fair and the serial killer who prowled it. True crime meets narrative history.

column1: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
column5: The story of the Black woman whose cancer cells were taken without consent and became one of the most important tools in medicine.
The former First Lady's memoir of her journey from Chicago's South Side to the White House. Warm, honest, and inspiring.
A botanist and member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation weaves indigenous wisdom and Western science into a meditation on plants and reciprocity.
A Pulitzer-winning account of the sixth mass extinction ‚Äî the one humans are causing. Essential and urgent.
Twenty lessons from the twentieth century on how to resist authoritarianism. Short, urgent, and more relevant every day.
Two Harvard political scientists explain how elected leaders dismantle democracy from within ‚Äî and what history tells us about stopping them.
A historian traces the roots of anti-fascist organizing from the 1920s to today. Essential context for understanding the movement.
An economist argues that racism has cost white Americans too ‚Äî and that solidarity is the path to prosperity for everyone.
